content.text: Enterprises that adopt GenAI capabilities without a coordination function accumulate a portfolio of overlapping tools: multiple teams paying for different LLM APIs that serve the same purpose, an AI code assistant purchased team-by-team when an enterprise licence would be cheaper, and vendor relationships that were established before the internal developer platform existed. A tool inventory and overlap assessment is the structured process that makes this sprawl visible — and provides the evidence base for a consolidation decision.
Why GenAI Tool Sprawl Happens
GenAI tool adoption has specific characteristics that make sprawl more likely than in conventional software procurement:
Low barrier to trial.

Most LLM APIs accept a credit card and return a working API key in minutes. A developer can trial an AI tool without any procurement approval, then continue using it in production when the trial produces good results. By the time procurement notices, the tool is in the critical path.
Rapid vendor proliferation.

The GenAI vendor landscape grew from a handful of providers in 2022 to hundreds of vendors offering overlapping capabilities by 2025. Teams adopted different tools in the same quarter based on who they heard about first, not based on a coordinated strategy.
Capability-bundled tools.

Many GenAI products bundle multiple capabilities: a code assistant that also does documentation generation, an AI writing tool that also handles translation, a RAG framework that also provides evaluation infrastructure. These bundles make overlap less obvious — two tools look different in their marketing but serve the same developer workflow.
Shadow IT accelerated by GenAI hype.

Business units purchased AI tools independently during the peak of GenAI adoption pressure. Procurement teams were often too slow relative to the pace of demand, leading business unit leaders to bypass the approval process to remain competitive.
Building the Tool Inventory

The first step in a rationalization process is to know what tools actually exist in the organisation. Self-reported inventories are systematically incomplete — teams underreport tools they purchased without approval. The inventory must be built from multiple data sources.
Data Sources for Discovery
Source
What it reveals
Completeness
Finance / AP system
Vendors with active invoices or credit card charges matching known AI vendor names
High for tools over $500/month; misses free tiers and team credit card spend
SSO / identity provider (Okta, Azure AD)
Applications with SAML/OIDC integrations; reveals all tools where employees have SSO-linked accounts
High for browser-based tools; misses direct API use and tools with no SSO
Network proxy / firewall logs
Outbound HTTPS requests to known AI vendor API endpoints
High for API-calling applications; requires mapping IP/domain to vendor
Software asset management (SAM) tool
Installed applications on managed devices
High for desktop tools; misses cloud/web tools and personal devices
Developer secrets / API key scans
Repository scanning for API keys matching known AI vendor formats
Catches tools embedded in code; requires access to code repositories
Expense reports
Employee expense reimbursements mentioning AI tools
Low — only tools submitted for reimbursement; misses company card charges
Engineering team survey
Self-reported tool use across engineering and product teams
Fills gaps the automated sources miss; subjective and under-reports shadow IT
Inventory Schema

# inventory/tool_record.py
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class GenAIToolRecord:
    # Identity
    tool_id:        str          # auto-generated: genai-tool-NNN
    vendor_name:    str          # e.g. "Anthropic"
    product_name:   str          # e.g. "Claude API"
    description:    str          # one line: what the tool does

    # Capability classification (allows overlap detection)
    capabilities:   list[str]    # from: ["text_generation", "code_generation",
                                 #  "embeddings", "image_generation", "search_rag",
                                 #  "evaluations", "fine_tuning", "speech", "vision"]

    # Usage context
    using_teams:    list[str]    # team IDs
    use_cases:      list[str]    # free text: what each team uses it for
    in_production:  bool
    estimated_users: int

    # Financial
    contract_type:  str          # "api_pay_per_use" | "saas_subscription" | "enterprise_licence" | "free_tier"
    monthly_spend:  Optional[float]   # USD; None if not known
    contract_end:   Optional[date]

    # Compliance status
    due_diligence_done: bool
    dpa_signed:     bool
    approved_for_prod: bool

    # Discovery metadata
    discovery_sources: list[str]  # which sources found this tool
    discovered_date: date
    inventory_owner: str          # person responsible for keeping this record current

@dataclass
class OverlapGroup:
    capability:     str
    tool_ids:       list[str]     # tools that all provide this capability
    recommended:    str           # which tool_id should be the standard
    consolidation_action: str     # "consolidate_to_recommended" | "maintain_both" | "deprecate_all"
    rationale:      str
Capability Overlap Detection

With a populated inventory, overlap detection identifies sets of tools where two or more active subscriptions cover the same capability. Not all overlaps are problems — an intentional multi-vendor strategy for resilience is not sprawl. The overlap assessment separates unintentional redundancy from intentional diversity.
The Overlap Assessment Matrix

The overlap assessment matrix is the output that the rationalization team presents to technology leadership. It shows each capability where overlap exists, the tools involved, the combined spend, and the recommended action.
Capability
Tools in overlap
Combined monthly spend
Teams affected
Compliance gaps
Recommended action
Text generation
Anthropic API + Azure OpenAI + AWS Bedrock
1 (Bedrock: no DPA)
Consolidate to internal platform gateway; Bedrock: complete DPA within 30 days or deprecate
Code generation
Azure OpenAI (via API) + GitHub Copilot
Maintain both: Copilot for IDE integration, Azure for programmatic use — differentiated use cases
Embeddings
Azure OpenAI + Cohere + internal sentence-transformers
1 (Cohere: no DPA)
Consolidate to Azure OpenAI for managed embeddings; deprecate Cohere (no DPA); keep sentence-transformers for offline/on-prem use
Evaluations
Custom scripts (12 teams) + internal eval runner
Migrate custom scripts to internal eval runner; no additional spend but reduces fragmentation
Classifying Tools for Rationalization Action

Each overlapping tool is classified into one of four rationalization actions. The classification is based on the tool's compliance status, the distinctiveness of its capabilities relative to other tools in the overlap group, and the migration cost.
Classification
Criteria
Action
Timeline
Keep as primary
Best capability fit; approved and compliant; majority of active teams already use it
Designate as the standard for this capability; route new teams to this tool
Immediate — communicate in next platform digest
Keep with scope limit
Serves a differentiated use case that the primary tool does not cover; compliant; limited team overlap
Define the approved use case; document the boundary; do not expand beyond that use case
Within 1 quarter — document and communicate boundary
Migrate to primary
Functionally overlaps with the primary tool; teams can migrate without significant friction; compliant
Set a migration deadline; support teams with migration guides; terminate contract at next renewal
1–2 quarters for migration; contract termination at renewal
Deprecate immediately
Failed or pending due diligence; no DPA; security compliance gap; no approved use case justification
Suspend new usage immediately; existing users migrate within 60 days; contract terminated
60-day hard cutoff; no extensions

Tools with compliance gaps — no due diligence, no DPA, or unapproved for production — are not simply candidates for consolidation. They are an active compliance risk. The inventory is not complete until every tool in the 'deprecate immediately' classification is actually off-boarded, and every team is confirmed to have migrated.
Inventory Maintenance

A tool inventory conducted once and not maintained becomes stale within months. New tools appear, vendor contracts change, and teams spin up new use cases without notifying procurement. The inventory must be a living document with a defined maintenance cadence.
Quarterly re-scan of all data sources (finance, SSO, proxy logs, secret scanning). New tools found in the scan are added to the inventory and flagged for compliance review.
Any new AI tool procurement requires an inventory update before or at the same time as contract signature. The inventory record is a procurement deliverable, not an afterthought.

Teams must notify the platform team when they start using a new AI capability or vendor, even during free-tier evaluation. A free-tier tool that transitions to paid without notification is a procurement compliance violation.
The inventory is reviewed at every quarterly rationalization review. Tools that have been marked for migration but have not been migrated have their deadline accelerated.
Automating the Inventory Refresh

Manual inventory surveys are reliable for the initial assessment but too slow for quarterly maintenance. The refresh cycle should be automated from the same data sources used in the original discovery.

# inventory/refresh.py
import csv
import re
from datetime import date, timedelta
from pathlib import Path

# ── Known AI vendor API endpoint suffixes (for proxy log matching) ──────────
AI_VENDOR_DOMAINS = {
    "api.anthropic.com":    "Anthropic",
    "api.openai.com":       "OpenAI (direct)",
    "openai.azure.com":     "Azure OpenAI",
    "bedrock-runtime.":     "AWS Bedrock",        # prefix match
    "api.cohere.ai":        "Cohere",
    "generativelanguage.googleapis.com": "Google Gemini",
    "api.together.xyz":     "Together AI",
    "api.mistral.ai":       "Mistral AI",
}

def classify_domain(domain: str) -> str | None:
    for pattern, vendor in AI_VENDOR_DOMAINS.items():
        if pattern in domain:
            return vendor
    return None

def scan_proxy_logs(log_file: Path, lookback_days: int = 30) -> dict[str, int]:
    """
    Parse proxy NCSA-format logs for outbound calls to AI vendor endpoints.
    Returns dict vendor -> request_count for the lookback window.
    """
    cutoff = date.today() - timedelta(days=lookback_days)
    vendor_counts: dict[str, int] = {}

    with log_file.open() as f:
        for line in f:
            parts = line.split()
            if len(parts) < 7:
                continue
            url = parts[6]
            # Extract domain from URL
            m = re.match(r"https?://([^/]+)", url)
            if not m:
                continue
            vendor = classify_domain(m.group(1))
            if vendor:
                vendor_counts[vendor] = vendor_counts.get(vendor, 0) + 1

    return vendor_counts

def scan_finance_export(csv_file: Path) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Parse AP / expense-management CSV for AI vendor charges.
    Expects columns: vendor_name, amount_usd, invoice_date, cost_center, po_number
    """
    ai_keywords = re.compile(
        r"anthropic|openai|azure ai|copilot|bedrock|cohere|mistral|together|"
        r"replicate|hugging.?face|stability.?ai|midjourney|runway|perplexity",
        re.IGNORECASE,
    )
    found = []
    with csv_file.open() as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            if ai_keywords.search(row.get("vendor_name", "")):
                found.append({
                    "vendor":       row["vendor_name"],
                    "amount_usd":   float(row.get("amount_usd", 0)),
                    "invoice_date": row.get("invoice_date"),
                    "cost_center":  row.get("cost_center"),
                })
    return found

def merge_findings(
    proxy_findings: dict[str, int],
    finance_findings: list[dict],
    existing_inventory: list[GenAIToolRecord],
) -> list[str]:
    """
    Compare new scan findings against the current inventory.
    Returns a list of vendor names that appear in scans but have no inventory record.
    """
    known_vendors = {t.vendor_name.lower() for t in existing_inventory}
    new_vendors = set()

    for vendor in proxy_findings:
        if vendor.lower() not in known_vendors:
            new_vendors.add(vendor)

    for row in finance_findings:
        v = row["vendor"].lower()
        # Fuzzy-match against known vendors
        if not any(v in known or known in v for known in known_vendors):
            new_vendors.add(row["vendor"])

    return sorted(new_vendors)
Rationalization Governance Roles
The overlap assessment produces findings, but findings do not produce rationalization without an explicit governance structure that assigns decision rights, escalation paths, and accountability.
Role
Responsibility in rationalization
Decision authority
GenAI Platform Lead
Owns the inventory; chairs quarterly rationalization review; signs off on 'keep' and 'scope-limit' decisions; escalates 'deprecate immediately' cases that teams resist
Full authority on platform-standard designation
Legal / Privacy
Reviews DPA status of every tool in inventory; approves 'deprecate immediately' classification for tools with no DPA; reviews contract terms for portability and exit
Authority to block continued production use pending DPA completion
CISO / Security
Reviews security questionnaire status; flags tools not cleared by security due diligence; approves migration timelines for tools with access to sensitive data
Authority to mandate immediate suspension for high-severity security gaps
Finance / Procurement
Validates spend data from AP system; executes contract terminations; negotiates enterprise licences to replace point subscriptions post-rationalization
Authority to refuse payment on invoices for tools not in approved inventory
Engineering Team Leads
Represent team-level usage in the rationalization review; own team-side migration effort for deprecated tools; sign off on migration completion
Can request exceptions with business case; exceptions require Platform Lead approval
Business Unit Sponsor
Approves business case for new tool additions; provides budget authority for enterprise licence upgrades when consolidation requires upgraded tier
Approval authority for exceptions and budget commitments above $50k/year
Tool Registration Workflow

The rationalization effort is undermined if new tools continue to enter production without inventory registration. A defined registration workflow, enforced at the procurement and infrastructure layers, prevents new sprawl from accumulating.
Team submits AI Tool Request.

A lightweight form captures vendor name, capability needed, estimated users, monthly spend estimate, and intended use cases. This triggers a Jira ticket tagged 'AI-TOOL-REQUEST' and automatically creates a draft GenAIToolRecord in the inventory system.
Overlap check runs automatically.
On ticket creation, the inventory system runs
detect_overlaps()

against the draft record's declared capabilities. If any existing tool in the inventory covers the same capability, the ticket is annotated with the overlap and the team is asked to confirm they have evaluated the existing tool and found it insufficient.
Security and Privacy review.

Legal and Security receive a notification. The security team kicks off the vendor security questionnaire (or confirms it is already on file). Legal confirms whether a DPA is required and whether one exists. Both teams must mark the ticket complete before approval.
Platform Lead decision.

With overlap analysis and compliance status in hand, the Platform Lead approves, approves-with-scope-limit, or rejects the request. Rejections include a pointer to the existing approved tool that covers the capability.
Inventory record confirmed.
On approval, the draft record is confirmed in the inventory. Finance is notified to add the vendor to the approved-vendor list. The tool is now trackable in quarterly re-scans.
Quarterly review cycle.

Every confirmed tool is reviewed quarterly. Tools that have had zero active users for 60 days are flagged for potential deprecation. Spend anomalies (actual vs. estimated > 2×) are escalated to Finance and the Business Unit Sponsor.
Common Inventory Assessment Mistakes
Treating the first assessment as a one-time project.

The inventory is a continuous capability, not a project deliverable. Teams that run an assessment, produce a report, and then let the inventory go stale will find themselves with worse sprawl 12 months later because the baseline created the illusion that the problem was solved.
Relying solely on self-reporting.

Engineering teams will report the tools they know they should have approval for. They will not report the tools they know they should not. Only cross-referencing finance, proxy logs, and secret scanning surfaces the full picture.
Conflating capability with product.

Two tools that look different in their marketing materials may serve identical purposes in practice. The inventory schema must classify tools by capability (text generation, embeddings, etc.) not by product name to make overlap visible.
Skipping compliance status in the overlap assessment.

An overlap between two fully compliant tools is a cost and efficiency problem. An overlap that includes a tool with no DPA or failed security review is also a legal and security problem. The assessment must surface compliance status alongside financial data.
Setting migration deadlines without supporting teams.

Rationalization decisions that designate a tool for migration fail when teams are given a deadline but no migration guide, no support from the platform team, and no escalation path when they hit technical blockers. Migration completion requires active support, not just a deadline.
Summary

GenAI tool sprawl is a predictable consequence of rapid adoption without coordination. A structured inventory — built from multiple data sources, not self-reported surveys alone — reveals the true extent of tool proliferation and compliance gaps. The capability overlap detection process identifies which tools serve the same function and scores the overlaps by combined spend, team count, and compliance risk. The overlap assessment matrix translates findings into prioritised rationalization actions: consolidate, scope-limit, migrate, or deprecate immediately. Automated refresh cycles, a defined registration workflow enforced at procurement, and explicit governance roles with clear decision rights prevent the assessment from becoming a one-time exercise that is obsolete within months.
